title: The Role of a 'Contact Person' for an Estonian e-Resident Company

author.name: Elena Petrova
author.bio: An international business lawyer by trade, Elena breaks down the complexities of freelance contracts, corporate structures, and international liability. Her goal is to empower freelancers with the legal knowledge to operate confidently.

reviewedBy.name: Priya Singh, Esq.
reviewedBy.bio: Priya is an attorney specializing in international contract law for independent contractors. She ensures that the legal advice provided is accurate, actionable, and up-to-date with current regulations.
credentials: Member of the New York State Bar

key_takeaways: Maintain a licensed Estonian contact person at all times to avoid the existential risk of compulsory dissolution, the legal termination of your company.
Vet potential providers by verifying their professional license, demanding a strict Service Level Agreement (SLA) for communications, and confirming transparent, all-inclusive annual fees.
Recognize that your contact person is a passive receiver of official documents, not a director, and has no authority to make business decisions or sign contracts.
Treat your choice of contact person as a strategic decision that signals your company's credibility to banks and partners, directly impacting your access to financial services.
summary: For Estonian e-residents, appointing a contact person is a critical decision, as a poor choice creates an existential risk of missed legal notices, fines, or even compulsory dissolution of your company. The article advises treating this legal requirement not as a low-cost compliance task but as the selection of a strategic partner, thoroughly vetting providers on their communication, competence, and cost-clarity. By making a deliberate choice, you transform this obligation into a powerful defense that safeguards your company's integrity and provides the peace of mind to focus on growth.
ai_overview_answer: An Estonian contact person is a legally mandated representative required for any company whose management board is located outside of Estonia, such as those founded through e-Residency. Their primary function is to serve as the official recipient for procedural documents and legal notices from Estonian state authorities, ensuring the company never misses critical correspondence. Failing to appoint and maintain a licensed contact person is a serious compliance breach that can lead to fines or even the compulsory dissolution of the company. As of 2023, only specific licensed professionals, such as notaries, law firms, or authorized trust and company service providers, are legally permitted to fill this role. When selecting a provider, it is crucial to verify their license, demand a clear Service Level Agreement (SLA) for handling mail, and ensure transparent, all-inclusive annual pricing, which typically ranges from €250 to €500. This role is strictly for receiving communications and holds no authority to make business decisions or sign contracts on the company's behalf.

itemListElement.name: The Role of a 'Contact Person' for an Estonian e-Resident Company

@graph.name: How to Appoint a Contact Person for an Estonian Company
@graph.headline: The Role of a 'Contact Person' for an Estonian e-Resident Company
@graph.description: A step-by-step guide to formally appointing a licensed contact person for your Estonian OÜ in the e-Business Register.

step.name: Execute the Service Agreement
step.text: Before you log into any state portal, you must have a legally binding service agreement signed with your chosen provider. This document is your foundational shield, outlining their duties, service level commitments, and a transparent fee structure. Once signed, your provider will give you their exact legal name and registry code. Guard this information—precision is absolutely critical for the online submission.

step.name: Prepare Your Digital Identity Toolkit
step.text: To make any official change to your Estonian OÜ, you must prove your identity as a director with unquestionable authority. This is the core function of your digital ID. Before you begin, ensure your tools are ready and functional: either your e-Residency card and a compatible card reader, or your pre-configured Smart-ID or Mobile-ID application. These are the sovereign keys that unlock the e-Business Register.

step.name: Navigate the e-Business Register to Initiate the Change
step.text: With your provider's details at hand and your digital ID active, navigate to the Estonian e-Business Register portal. Log in using your preferred secure authentication method. Once inside, select your company under 'My undertakings.' Click the button clearly labeled 'Change the data.' From the application menu, navigate to the section labeled 'Persons.'

step.name: Enter Details, Upload Consent, and Digitally Sign
step.text: In the 'Persons' section, you will find the option to add or edit the contact person. Carefully enter the registry code and legal name of your new provider exactly as they were given to you. Your new provider must also supply you with a digitally signed consent form, typically an .asice file. You will upload this document in the 'Documents' section of the application. After reviewing all information for 100% accuracy, proceed to the final action: signing the application with your digital ID. The register typically processes the change within one to five business days.

mainEntity.name: What is the average cost of a contact person service in Estonia?

acceptedAnswer.text: For a fully licensed, professional provider, you should budget between €250 and €500 annually. This fee typically bundles the mandatory contact person service with a legal registered address. Be extremely cautious of offers significantly below this price range, as they can be a red flag for poor quality, hidden fees, or an unlicensed provider.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between a legal address and a contact person?

acceptedAnswer.text: These two concepts are distinct but related. A legal address is the official registered address of your company in Estonia, mandatory for every OÜ. A contact person is a separate requirement specifically for companies whose management board is located outside of Estonia. This person is legally empowered to receive official documents on the company's behalf. While providers often bundle them, they fulfill different legal functions.

mainEntity.name: Can my friend who lives in Estonia be my contact person?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. As of February 1, 2023, the law is clear: the role of a contact person can only be filled by a select group of licensed professionals. This list includes notaries, law firms, sworn auditors, and licensed trust and company service providers. This regulation ensures that the entity receiving your critical legal documents is qualified, accountable, and subject to professional oversight.

mainEntity.name: How do I choose a reliable contact person in Estonia?

acceptedAnswer.text: Your selection process should be guided by due diligence, not just price. Prioritize licensed providers, demand a clear Service Level Agreement (SLA) for handling correspondence, and look for a transparent, flat annual fee. For a detailed breakdown of evaluation criteria, please refer to the 'CEO's Vetting Checklist' section above.

mainEntity.name: What happens if my company doesn't have a contact person?

acceptedAnswer.text: The consequences are severe. Failing to appoint and maintain a licensed contact person when one is legally required is a major compliance violation. The Commercial Register has the authority to issue warnings and fines. If the situation is not rectified, the register can initiate compulsory dissolution proceedings, effectively liquidating your company. This is not a risk worth taking.

body.content: Beyond the Legal Requirement: Your Company’s Gatekeeper

For the director of a global company, the term "contact person" can sound like a minor administrative detail—just another address for the file. But for an entrepreneur who has established an Estonian OÜ through e-Residency, this is one of the most critical appointments you will make. This is not just a box to tick during company formation. The Estonian company contact person is the legally mandated gatekeeper for your entire EU entity.

We understand the deep "compliance anxiety" that comes with navigating another country's regulations. The official language can feel dense, the stakes incredibly high. For any serious Business-of-One, the threat of fines or, in the worst case, "compulsory dissolution" for a compliance failure is a genuine fear. If the registrar cannot reach your company through a licensed contact person, they can initiate proceedings to have your company forcibly dissolved. This isn't a small risk; it's an existential one.

This guide is designed to cut through that anxiety. It is not another dry summary of the Estonian Commercial Code. Think of this as a CEO's playbook, a strategic framework for transforming this legal requirement from a passive burden into a powerful asset. By the end, you will have a clear methodology to select a partner who not only fulfills a legal mandate but actively protects your company, streamlines your operations, and provides you with invaluable peace of mind. Making the right choice here is your first real act of strategic defense in the EU market.
Why Your Contact Person is Your Company's First Line of Defense

This strategic defense begins by reframing the legal mandate itself. The Estonian Commercial Code requires a contact person for any company whose management board is located abroad. For most e-Residency founders, this applies directly to you. It’s easy to view this as just another bureaucratic hurdle. Don't. See it for what it truly is: a state-mandated shield. This isn't about adding a line item to your compliance checklist; it's about establishing a legally recognized channel for all critical communications from the Estonian government, ensuring your company remains in good standing.
The Gatekeeper Role Explained

At its core, your contact person’s primary duty is to receive procedural documents and official declarations on your company's behalf. Think of them as your company’s official, legally designated mailbox in Estonia. When the Estonian Tax and Customs Board, a court, or the Commercial Register itself needs to serve you with a notice, they send it to your contact person. Delivery to them is legally considered delivery to you. Their reliability becomes your reliability. A time-sensitive tax audit notification or a critical legal summons cannot afford to get lost in the complexities of international post. A professional gatekeeper on the ground ensures you are never blindsided by a deadline you didn't know you had.
Connecting to Broader Operational Integrity

Beyond the strict legal function, appointing a reputable, professional contact person sends a powerful signal to the entire business ecosystem. Financial institutions, potential partners, and regulators see this choice as a reflection of your company's character. It demonstrates a serious commitment to legal compliance and sound governance. This single decision can make ancillary processes, like opening a business bank account or securing a contract, significantly smoother. It builds a foundation of trust and shows that even though you operate globally, your company is managed with precision and respect for local regulations.
What Your Contact Person Is—and Is Not

Finally, it is absolutely critical to understand the limits of this role to avoid dangerous assumptions. Your contact person is a passive receiver of information, not an active participant in your business. Confusing this role with a director or a legal representative is a common and costly mistake. Their function is vital but strictly defined.

rows: Receive official & procedural documents from Estonian authorities.
Make business decisions on your behalf.
Forward all correspondence to you per your service agreement.
Sign contracts or enter into agreements for the company.
Provide a registered legal address for your Estonian OÜ.
Act as a director, board member, or shareholder.
Confirm to authorities that they are your designated contact.
Represent your company in business negotiations or legal proceedings.
Hold a specific professional license (e.g., trust service provider).
Manage your company's finances or access its bank accounts.

body.content: Their role is one of immense responsibility, not authority. Recognizing this distinction is the first step in leveraging the position correctly and safeguarding your business.
The Three Hidden Risks of Choosing the Wrong Contact Person

The consequences of getting this decision wrong are not merely administrative—they are existential threats. Choosing an inadequate or unprofessional contact person exposes your Estonian OÜ to three severe, yet often overlooked, risks.
Risk #1: The Operational Black Hole of Missed Communications

Imagine you are traveling for a critical business expansion. Meanwhile, a time-sensitive notice from the Estonian Tax and Customs Board arrives at your contact person's office. A cheap, unresponsive provider fails to scan and forward it promptly. Days, or even weeks, pass. By the time you receive the notification, you have missed a non-negotiable deadline. This single failure can trigger fines, initiate a tax audit, or lead to other penalties. Your business is now in jeopardy because your legally-mandated gatekeeper was asleep at the post.
Risk #2: The Compliance Domino Effect into Legal Jeopardy

Far more dangerous than a missed notice is the complete failure to maintain a valid contact person. This is not a minor infraction. If your provider's license lapses, or if your service agreement expires and is not renewed, your company is in direct breach of the Estonian Commercial Code. The process is chillingly efficient. The Commercial Register will issue a warning and provide a deadline to appoint a new, valid contact person. If you fail to comply, the register can initiate compulsory dissolution proceedings. This is the corporate equivalent of a death sentence—a formal, court-ordered process to liquidate your company and legally erase its existence.
Risk #3: The Reputational Red Flag That Damages Credibility

Your choice of contact person sends a clear signal to the financial ecosystem. When you apply to open a business bank account or seek a partnership, institutions perform due diligence. They scrutinize every aspect of your company's setup, including your registered address and contact person. Appointing a provider known for low quality, or one that isn't properly licensed, acts as a significant red flag. To a bank's risk assessment team, this suggests a casual approach to legal compliance. This perception alone can lead to enhanced scrutiny, protracted onboarding, or an outright denial of essential services.
The CEO's Vetting Checklist: How to Choose a Strategic Partner

Avoiding these risks requires a deliberate shift in mindset: you are not merely buying a compliance product, but selecting a strategic partner. The quality of your contact person directly reflects the operational integrity of your company. Instead of letting price be the sole driver, a thoughtful CEO evaluates potential partners using a simple but powerful framework: **The Three C's of Vetting.**
The Three C's: Communication, Competence, and Cost-Clarity
This framework cuts through marketing noise and focuses on what actually preserves your company's good standing.

items: **Communication:** This is the most critical function. A low-cost provider might scan your mail "when they get to it." A professional partner, however, operates on a strict Service Level Agreement (SLA). You should expect, and demand, a guarantee that all official documents are scanned and forwarded to you within one business day. Ask to see their client portal. Is it a modern, secure dashboard, or a clunky, outdated system? This is your primary communication channel with the Estonian state—it needs to be flawless.

**Competence:** Your contact person must be legally authorized to perform the role. The Estonian Commercial Code is explicit: only notaries, law firms, sworn auditors, or service providers licensed under the Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing Prevention Act are permitted. Ask for their license number and verify it in the official Register of Economic Activities (MTR). Beyond the license, consider their experience. How long have they specialized in e-Residency services? A deep portfolio demonstrates a robust understanding of the ecosystem and its potential pitfalls.

**Cost-Clarity:** Professional service providers offer transparent, all-inclusive annual fees. You should expect to invest between €250 and €500 per year for a high-quality, bundled legal address and contact person service. Be extremely wary of prices significantly below this range. They often conceal hidden fees for basic tasks like scanning per page, mail forwarding, or handling "excessive" volume. A trustworthy partner puts everything in a clear agreement.

body.content: Red Flags and Critical Questions
As you evaluate options, watch for these warning signs and ask these direct questions before you commit.

rows: Pricing that seems too good to be true.
Transparent, all-inclusive annual pricing.
Inability to produce a valid license number.
Proactively displays their license number.
Vague answers about document handling.
A clear, documented Service Level Agreement (SLA).
No formal service agreement defining responsibilities.
A comprehensive service agreement.

body.content: **Ask these questions directly:**

items: "Can you please provide your license number as a Trust and Company Service Provider?"
"What is your exact, documented procedure and Service Level Agreement for receiving, scanning, and notifying me of official correspondence?"
"Is your annual fee all-inclusive? Please provide a detailed list of any and all potential extra charges."
"Beyond the contact person service, what other compliance and advisory support can you offer as my company scales?"

body.content: Their answers will reveal everything you need to know about their professionalism and whether they are a genuine partner capable of protecting your business.
Appointing Your Contact Person: A Step-by-Step Guide

With your rigorous vetting complete and a trusted partner selected, the final action is to formalize their appointment in the state's e-Business Register. Estonia's world-class digital infrastructure makes the process remarkably transparent and secure. Executing this change correctly is the definitive step that solidifies your company’s legal compliance.
Think of this process as digitally notarizing the role of your company's legal gatehouse. The following four steps ensure you do it correctly.

items:
**Step 1: Execute the Service Agreement**
Before you log into any state portal, you must have a legally binding service agreement signed with your chosen provider. This document is your foundational shield, outlining their duties, service level commitments, and a transparent fee structure. Once signed, your provider will give you their exact legal name and registry code. Guard this information—precision is absolutely critical for the online submission.

**Step 2: Prepare Your Digital Identity Toolkit**
To make any official change to your Estonian OÜ, you must prove your identity as a director with unquestionable authority. This is the core function of your digital ID. Before you begin, ensure your tools are ready and functional: either your e-Residency card and a compatible card reader, or your pre-configured Smart-ID or Mobile-ID application. These are the sovereign keys that unlock the e-Business Register.

**Step 3: Navigate the e-Business Register to Initiate the Change**
With your provider's details at hand and your digital ID active, navigate to the Estonian e-Business Register portal.
1.  Log in using your preferred secure authentication method.
2.  Once inside, select your company under "My undertakings."
3.  Click the button clearly labeled **"Change the data."**
4.  From the application menu, navigate to the section labeled **"Persons."**

**Step 4: Enter Details, Upload Consent, and Digitally Sign**
In the "Persons" section, you will find the option to add or edit the contact person. Carefully enter the registry code and legal name of your new provider exactly as they were given to you. Your new provider must also supply you with a digitally signed consent form, typically an .asice file. You will upload this document in the "Documents" section of the application. After reviewing all information for 100% accuracy, proceed to the final action: signing the application with your digital ID. The register typically processes the change within one to five business days.

body.content: Frequently Asked Questions
Mastering the details is key to confident corporate governance. Here are direct answers to the most common queries we hear from global professionals like you.

items:
**What is the average cost of a contact person service in Estonia?**
For a fully licensed, professional provider, you should budget between €250 and €500 annually. This fee typically bundles the mandatory contact person service with a legal registered address. Be extremely cautious of offers significantly below this price range, as they can be a red flag for poor quality, hidden fees, or an unlicensed provider.

**What is the difference between a legal address and a contact person?**
These two concepts are distinct but related. A **legal address** is the official registered address of your company in Estonia, mandatory for every OÜ. A **contact person** is a separate requirement specifically for companies whose management board is located outside of Estonia. This person is legally empowered to receive official documents on the company's behalf. While providers often bundle them, they fulfill different legal functions.

**Can my friend who lives in Estonia be my contact person?**
No. As of February 1, 2023, the law is clear: the role of a contact person can only be filled by a select group of licensed professionals. This list includes notaries, law firms, sworn auditors, and licensed trust and company service providers. This regulation ensures that the entity receiving your critical legal documents is qualified, accountable, and subject to professional oversight.

**How do I choose a reliable contact person in Estonia?**
Your selection process should be guided by due diligence, not just price. Prioritize licensed providers, demand a clear Service Level Agreement (SLA) for handling correspondence, and look for a transparent, flat annual fee. For a detailed breakdown of evaluation criteria, please refer to the "CEO's Vetting Checklist" section above.

**What happens if my company doesn't have a contact person?**
The consequences are severe. Failing to appoint and maintain a licensed contact person when one is legally required is a major compliance violation. The Commercial Register has the authority to issue warnings and fines. If the situation is not rectified, the register can initiate compulsory dissolution proceedings, effectively liquidating your company. This is not a risk worth taking.

body.content: From Compliance Burden to Strategic Confidence

It's easy to see this appointment as a rigid obligation. But this framework is not designed to be a burden; it is engineered to be your shield. The legal mandate for a licensed professional ensures a steady, reliable channel of communication, protecting you from catastrophic risk. This isn't just about ticking a box—it is your first and most critical strategic decision in safeguarding your company's operational integrity.
Embracing this perspective requires a fundamental shift in mindset from a founder burdened by rules to a CEO who commands them.

rows: **Focus:** Minimizing annual fees above all else.
**Focus:** Maximizing reliability and peace of mind.
**Vetting:** A quick search for the lowest price.
**Vetting:** Thorough evaluation of competence, communication (SLAs), and cost clarity.
**Outcome:** A passive, often unresponsive gatekeeper.
**Outcome:** An active, professional shield that ensures no critical correspondence is ever missed.
**Risk Profile:** High. Exposes the company to missed deadlines, fines, and dissolution.
**Risk Profile:** Low. Builds a foundation of trust and demonstrates a serious commitment to governance.

body.content: Choosing your contact person is the ultimate act of foresight. By shifting your focus from minimizing a small annual cost to vetting a crucial long-term partner, you build a stronger, more resilient business from day one. A well-chosen provider operates silently and efficiently in the background, giving you the confidence and peace of mind necessary to focus on what you actually do best: running and growing your business. Make this choice deliberately. Your future CEO-self will thank you.
